lessons.title: Predicate<T>
lessons.summary: Learn how to use the test() method of the Predicate<T> interface, the default methods and(), or(), and negate(), and the static method isEqual().

contents.title: What is Predicate<T>?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The basics of [[Predicate]]<T> (a function that performs a conditional test)
- Executing tests with the test() [[method]]
- Composing conditions with and(), or(), and negate()

This is the first step toward **functional programming**, where "conditions" can be treated as values.

contents.title: Basics of the test() Method
contents.body:
The abstract method of Predicate<T> is test(T t), which takes one argument and returns a boolean.
As a [[functional interface]], it can be implemented with [[lambda expressions]] or [[method references]].
Predicate<String> performs a conditional test on a string, while Predicate<Integer> performs a test on an integer.
[[Primitive types]] cannot be specified for the type parameter T; [[wrapper classes]] must be used instead.
Primitive specializations such as IntPredicate, LongPredicate, and DoublePredicate are also provided.
contents.note: Predicate<T> is used as an argument for Stream.filter() and Collection.removeIf().

contents.title: Composition with and(), or(), and negate()
contents.body:
**Predicate** has three default methods: and(), or(), and negate().
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| and() | Corresponds to logical AND (&&); returns true if both conditions are satisfied. |
| or() | Corresponds to logical OR (\|\|); returns true if either condition is satisfied. |
| negate() | Corresponds to negation (!); inverts the boolean result of the condition. |
These methods return a Predicate, so they can be chained to build complex conditions.
Evaluation uses short-circuit evaluation: and() stops at the first false, and or() stops at the first true.

contents.title: The Predicate.isEqual() Static Method
contents.body:
Predicate.isEqual(targetRef) is a static method that returns a Predicate which tests whether an argument is equal to targetRef using equals().
If targetRef is null, it returns a Predicate that tests whether the argument is null.
Internally it uses Objects.equals(), enabling null-safe comparison.
This method makes it easy to create equality-testing Predicates concisely.
On the exam, the case of isEqual(null) may appear in questions.
contents.note: Predicate.not() was added in Java 11. It has the same effect as negate(), but has the advantage of being usable as a static method.

contents.title: Integrating Predicate with the Stream API
contents.body:
Predicate<T> is most frequently used as an argument to Stream.filter().
Defining conditions as Predicate variables makes them easy to reuse and compose.
Using Collection.removeIf(Predicate), you can delete all elements that match a condition in one operation.
Stream.allMatch(), anyMatch(), and noneMatch() also accept Predicates.
Externalizing conditions into variables improves testability and code readability.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: p3 is the and() composition of p1 and p2. p3.test("Java"): length() > 3 (4 > 3 = true) and startsWith("J") (true), so true. p3.test("Go"): length() > 3 (2 > 3 = false), so false. p3.test("JavaScript"): length() > 3 (10 > 3 = true) and startsWith("J") (true), so true.

quizzes.question: What is the return value when null is passed to a Predicate generated by Predicate.isEqual(null)?
quizzes.explanation: Predicate.isEqual(null) returns a Predicate that tests whether the argument is null. Internally, Objects.equals(null, null) is called, which returns true. isEqual() performs null-safe equality testing.

quizzes.question: The default method that negates (inverts) a Predicate's condition is ____().
quizzes.explanation: The negate() default method of Predicate returns a new Predicate that inverts the result of the original Predicate. For example, the negate() of isEmpty behaves equivalently to isNotEmpty. Since Java 11, the Predicate.not() static method is also available.

lessons.title: Function<T,R> and UnaryOperator<T>
lessons.summary: Learn the apply() method of Function<T,R>, the difference between the composition methods andThen() and compose(), and the characteristics of UnaryOperator<T>.

contents.title: What is Function<T,R>?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to use [[Function]]<T,R> (a function that performs [[type conversion]])
- The difference from [[UnaryOperator]]<T> (conversion within the same type)
- Chaining functions with andThen() and compose()

You can build pipelines that transform data.

contents.title: Basics of the apply() Method
contents.body:
The abstract [[method]] of Function<T,R> is apply(T t), which takes an argument of type T and returns a result of type R.
For example, Function<String, Integer> is a function that takes a string and returns an integer.
It can be implemented with [[lambda expressions]] or [[method references]], and can be written concisely as String::length using a method reference.
Primitive specializations such as IntFunction<R>, ToIntFunction<T>, and IntToDoubleFunction are provided.
Using these specializations can avoid the cost of [[autoboxing]].

contents.title: The Difference Between andThen() and compose()
contents.body:
andThen() performs composition by applying the current function first, then applying the specified function to the result.
compose() applies the specified function first, then applies the current function to that result.
In other words, f.andThen(g) is the same as g(f(x)), and f.compose(g) is the same as f(g(x)).
It is easier to understand if you remember that andThen() means "after this function" and compose() means "before this function".
On exams, the difference in application order between andThen() and compose() is frequently tested.
Both methods return a new Function, so the original Function is not modified.
contents.note: andThen(g): this → g order. compose(g): g → this order. Pay attention to the order!

contents.title: Function.identity() and UnaryOperator<T>
contents.body:
Function.identity() is a static method that returns an identity function which returns its argument unchanged.
It is useful when you want to use the value as-is, such as in the valueMapper of Stream.toMap().
UnaryOperator<T> is an [[interface]] that [[inherits]] from Function<T,T> and is used when the input and output are the same type.
Since UnaryOperator is a sub-interface of Function<T,T>, it inherits the apply() method.
List.replaceAll() takes a UnaryOperator<E> as its argument.
contents.note: Since UnaryOperator<T> inherits from Function<T,T>, it can be used wherever a **Function** is required.

contents.title: Practical Patterns for Function Composition
contents.body:
By leveraging [[function composition]], you can build data transformation pipelines declaratively.
Chaining multiple Functions with andThen() allows you to describe step-by-step transformations clearly.
Passing a composed function to Stream.map() can reduce the number of [[intermediate operations]].
**Composition** is [[lazily evaluated]], so no actual computation occurs until apply() is called.
Function composition improves reusability and makes testing easier.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: f.andThen(g).apply(5) first computes f(5) = 15, then g(15) = 45. f.compose(g).apply(5) first computes g(5) = 15, then f(15) = 25. andThen() applies in the order f → g, while compose() applies in the order g → f.

quizzes.question: Which of the following statements about UnaryOperator<T> is correct?
quizzes.explanation: UnaryOperator<T> is an interface that extends Function<T,T>. It represents a function where the input and output are the same type T. Since it is a sub-interface of Function<T,T>, it inherits the apply() method. List.replaceAll() uses UnaryOperator<E> as its argument.

choices.text: It is an interface that extends Function<T,T>
It is an interface that extends Consumer<T>
It represents a function where the input and output types differ
It has execute() as its abstract method

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: tripleLen = len.andThen(triple) first applies len (string length), then applies triple (multiply by 3) to the result. The length of "Java" is 4, 4 * 3 = 12. The length of "Hi" is 2, 2 * 3 = 6.

quizzes.question: Complete the code that converts a string to an integer using the Function interface.
quizzes.explanation: Function<T,R> is a functional interface that takes an argument of type T and returns a result of type R. It is executed with apply().

lessons.title: Consumer<T> and Supplier<T>
lessons.summary: Learn the accept() method and andThen() composition of Consumer<T>, as well as the get() method and lazy evaluation mechanism of Supplier<T>.

contents.title: The Roles of Consumer and Supplier
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to use [[Consumer]]<T> (a function that consumes a value)
- How to use [[Supplier]]<T> (a function that supplies a value)
- Combining with forEach()

Express **side-effect** operations and value generation as functions.

contents.title: Consumer<T>'s accept() and andThen()
contents.body:
The abstract [[method]] of Consumer<T> is accept(T t), which takes an argument and performs an operation, but has no return value (void).
Consumer is used as the argument for Iterable.forEach() and Stream.forEach().
Multiple Consumers can be chained using the default method andThen().
Consumer<T> does not have or() or negate(). Logical operations are not possible because there is no return value.
Primitive specializations IntConsumer, LongConsumer, and DoubleConsumer are also available.
contents.note: Consumer<T> does not have or() or negate(). Only andThen() is available.

contents.title: The get() Method of Supplier<T>
contents.body:
The abstract method of Supplier<T> is get(), which takes no arguments and returns a value of type T.
It is used for object creation and providing default values.
**Optional**.orElseGet() takes a Supplier as its argument and generates a default value with [[lazy evaluation]].
A [[constructor]] reference (e.g., [[ArrayList]]::new) can be used as a Supplier.
Using orElseGet() instead of orElse() avoids unnecessary computation when a value is present.
contents.note: Using orElseGet(**Supplier**) avoids unnecessary computation when a value is present.

contents.title: Practical Uses of Consumer and Supplier
contents.body:
**Consumer** is used for operations on each element of a collection (log output, saving to DB, etc.).
Supplier excels in the lazy initialization pattern, deferring object creation until it is needed.
Stream.generate(Supplier) can create an infinite stream.
Objects.requireNonNull(obj, Supplier) allows lazy generation of the error message when null.
Usage like Logger.info(() -> "expensive message generation") generates the message only when the log level is enabled.

contents.title: Functional Interface Reference Table
contents.body:
Let's organize the key [[functional interfaces]] in the java.util.function package.
| Interface | Description |
|---|---|
| Predicate<T> | T → boolean (conditional test); abstract method is test(). |
| Function<T,R> | T → R (transformation); abstract method is apply(). |
| Consumer<T> | T → void (consumption); abstract method is accept(). |
| Supplier<T> | () → T (supply); abstract method is get(). |
Accurately memorizing the method names and argument/return type combinations for each [[interface]] is key to passing the Gold exam.
contents.note: On the exam, you need to accurately memorize the abstract method name for each interface.

quizzes.question: Which is the correct abstract method of Supplier<T>?
quizzes.explanation: The abstract method of Supplier<T> is get(). It takes no arguments and returns a value of type T. Methods like supply(), provide(), and create() do not exist. Supplier is used for factory patterns and lazy evaluation.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: c3 is a Consumer composed by c1.andThen(c2). First, c1 is executed and "Java".toUpperCase() prints "JAVA". Then c2 is executed and "Java".length() prints 4. With Consumer's andThen(), the same argument "Java" is passed to both Consumers.

quizzes.question: The abstract method of Consumer<T> is ____(), which takes one argument and has no return value.
quizzes.explanation: The abstract method of Consumer<T> is accept(T t). It takes one argument and executes a process with no return value (void). It is used as the argument for Iterable.forEach() and Stream.forEach().

lessons.title: BiFunction, BiPredicate, BiConsumer
lessons.summary: Learn how to use the Bi-series functional interfaces that take two arguments, BinaryOperator<T>, and how to integrate them with Map.

contents.title: What are the Bi-series Interfaces?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- [[BiFunction]]<T,U,R> (a two-argument transformation function)
- BiPredicate<T,U> (a two-argument conditional test)
- BiConsumer<T,U> (a two-argument consuming function)

These are [[functional interfaces]] for scenarios that require two arguments.

contents.title: Basics of BiFunction<T,U,R>
contents.body:
The abstract [[method]] of BiFunction<T,U,R> is apply(T t, U u), which takes two arguments and returns a result of type R.
The default method andThen() can be used to apply further transformations to the result.
BiFunction does not have a compose() method (because the direction of composition is ambiguous with two arguments).
BiFunction is used as the argument for Map.compute(), Map.merge(), and Map.replaceAll().
BiPredicate<T,U> has the test(T t, U u) method and can be composed with and(), or(), and negate().
contents.note: BiFunction does not have a compose() method. Only andThen() is available.

contents.title: BiConsumer and Map.forEach
contents.body:
The abstract method of BiConsumer<T,U> is accept(T t, U u), which takes two arguments and performs a process with no return value.
Map.forEach() takes a BiConsumer<K,V> as its argument and executes a process for each entry.
BiConsumer also has the andThen() default method, allowing processes to be chained.
While the normal Consumer<T> takes one argument, BiConsumer<T,U> takes two arguments.
Note that the argument types differ between Map's forEach and List's forEach.

contents.title: BinaryOperator<T>
contents.body:
[[BinaryOperator]]<T> is an [[interface]] that [[inherits]] from BiFunction<T,T,T> and takes two arguments of the same type, returning a result of the same type.
BinaryOperator is used as the argument for Stream.reduce().
The static methods BinaryOperator.minBy([[Comparator]]) and maxBy(Comparator) are provided.
minBy() returns a BinaryOperator that returns the smaller of two values, and maxBy() returns the larger.
BinaryOperator is also used as the third argument (collision handler) of Map.merge().
contents.note: BinaryOperator<T> is a sub-interface of BiFunction<T,T,T>.

contents.title: Comparing Bi-series and Single-argument Interfaces
contents.body:
Each Bi-series interface has a corresponding single-argument version.
Function<T,R> → BiFunction<T,U,R>, Predicate<T> → BiPredicate<T,U>, Consumer<T> → BiConsumer<T,U> are the corresponding pairs.
Java does not provide standard functional interfaces that take three or more arguments.
When three or more arguments are needed, you can either define a custom functional interface or use currying.
The Gold exam covers up to the Bi-series interfaces.
contents.note: There are no standard functional interfaces for three or more arguments. Use custom definitions or currying.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: BinaryOperator.maxBy() generates a BinaryOperator that returns the larger value based on the Comparator. Since we compare by string length, "Java" (4) vs "Python" (6) returns "Python", and "Go" (2) vs "C" (1) returns "Go".

quizzes.question: Which default method is defined in the BiFunction interface?
quizzes.explanation: BiFunction<T,U,R> only has the andThen() default method defined. The compose() method is not provided because the direction of composition is ambiguous with two arguments. or() and negate() are methods of BiPredicate.

quizzes.question: The following code has a compile error. What is the cause of the error?
quizzes.code:
BiFunction<String, Integer, String> bf = (s, n) -> s.repeat(n);
BiFunction<String, Integer, Integer> result = bf.compose(String::length);
quizzes.explanation: BiFunction does not have a compose() method. BiFunction only supports andThen(). compose() is a default method defined only in the Function interface. BiFunction does not provide compose() because the direction of composition is indeterminate with two arguments.

choices.text: The compose() method does not exist in BiFunction
The return type of s.repeat(n) does not match
String::length cannot be used with BiFunction
Type inference for the lambda expression parameters fails

lessons.title: Function Composition and Chaining
lessons.summary: Learn Function composition patterns, complex filtering with Predicate chains, and how to build validation pipelines for practical use.

contents.title: The Concept of Function Composition
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to apply functions sequentially with andThen()
- How to apply functions in reverse order with compose()
- Composing [[Predicates]] (and, or, negate)

A technique for building complex operations by combining small functions.

contents.title: Building Function Chains
contents.body:
By calling andThen() in succession, you can build a multi-stage data transformation pipeline.
Each stage's function can be independently tested and reused.
Passing a composed function to Stream.map() can consolidate multiple map() calls into one.
**Composition** requires type consistency — the output type of the previous function must match the input type of the next function.
If the types don't match, a [[compile]] error occurs, ensuring type safety.

contents.title: Compound Conditions with Predicate Chains
contents.body:
By combining **Predicate**'s and(), or(), and negate(), you can declaratively build complex filtering conditions.
Extracting individual conditions as Predicate variables makes them easy to reuse and test.
An and() chain of multiple conditions is true when all conditions are met (logical AND), while an or() chain is true when any is met (logical OR).
Instead of parentheses, the order of and() and or() calls controls the evaluation order.
and() does not have higher precedence than or(), so you must compose in the intended order.
contents.note: The binding precedence of and() and or() differs from Java's && and ||; it follows the order of method calls.

contents.title: Building a Validation Pipeline
contents.body:
Using [[function composition]], you can build validation pipelines that progressively verify input values.
This is a pattern where Predicate chains define multiple validation conditions, and [[Function]] chains perform data transformations.
Separating validation and transformation makes the responsibility of each process clear.
Combining with the [[Stream API]] allows declarative description of bulk validation and transformation on large datasets.
In practice, this is used for validating user input and API request validation.

contents.title: Best Practices for Function Composition
contents.body:
Functions to be composed should ideally be **pure functions** without side effects.
Defining each function as a meaningfully named variable makes the code's intent clear.
If an andThen() chain becomes too long, split it with intermediate variables to maintain readability.
andThen() is generally preferred over compose() as it tends to be more readable.
Function composition delivers maximum benefit when combined with the Stream API.
contents.note: Keep functions to be composed as **pure functions** without side effects whenever possible. This makes testing and parallel processing easier.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: The pipeline applies trim → upper → len in order. "  Hello  " trimmed is "Hello", uppercased is "HELLO", length is 5. " Hi " trimmed is "Hi", uppercased is "HI", length is 2. andThen() applies from left to right.

quizzes.question: Which statement about the evaluation order of Predicate's and() and or() is correct?
quizzes.explanation: Predicate's and() and or() do not have operator-like precedence as in Java; they are evaluated in the order of method chain calls. p1.or(p2).and(p3) first evaluates p1.or(p2), then applies and(p3) to the result. This means (p1 || p2) && p3.

choices.text: Evaluated in method chain order, resulting in (p1 || p2) && p3
and() takes precedence over or(), resulting in p1 || (p2 && p3)
or() takes precedence over and(), same as (p1 || p2) && p3 but for a different reason
The evaluation order is nondeterministic and results may vary between executions

quizzes.question: Arrange the code in the correct order to build a pipeline of "trim → lowercase → get length" using Function.andThen().
quizzes.explanation: First, declare the Function variables. Starting from the trim function, compose with andThen() to add the lower function, then compose again with andThen() to add the length function. Finally, apply it with apply(). Since andThen() applies sequentially from left to right, processing occurs in the order trim → lower → length.
